title: A shorter Briefing: the detail folds away until you ask for it
note: The morning Briefing is about a fifth shorter without losing anything. The Macro Trigger Map's three always-open event cards are now one-line rows — event, date, and how many signals would move each way — that click open to the full bull/bear playbook. The AI Capex Pulse keeps its verdict, coverage-gap read, and four signal tiles on the page, but the history charts now live behind one 'History' expander. And in the Macro Note, the four 'what each scenario means' paragraphs fold under the scenario-odds bar, which already shows the probabilities. Every word is still there — it just waits until you ask.

title: New page: Retrospective — what we called, what happened
note: A new page tells each month's story in plain sentences: what we told you to buy or avoid, at what price and with what target, and how it worked out over the following 20 trading sessions — grouped into what worked, what didn't, and what's too early to judge, with the paper book's month alongside. Past months never get quietly rewritten, retired names stay on the record, and the single-regime caution banner sits above every month. Find it in the top navigation as 'Review'.

title: Readable on a phone: tables reflow, no sideways scrolling
note: On phone screens the wide tables no longer hang past the right edge with half their columns out of sight. The watchlist, the tracker's by-name ledger and its episode history, the paper-book position and trade tables, and the comparison-page diffs each reflow into stacked cards — every number sitting under its own small label — so a row reads top-to-bottom on one screen instead of swiping sideways. Desktop layouts are unchanged, and the comparison page's rationale snippets now end at a word with an ellipsis instead of cutting off mid-word.

title: Real positions: whole shares, cost basis, cash
note: Every positions table in the paper-book drawers now reads like a real brokerage account: whole-share counts for a $100,000 pot (rounded to the nearest share, spending a touch more or less), buy date @ average fill price, the current price, and cost → value in pot dollars — plus a per-book line showing the pot's current worth, cash, and open-position count (the extension lanes each carry their own compounding pot). The paper book's display pot is now $100,000 throughout, so whole shares of high-priced names stay honest.

title: Selling-on-extension trade history
note: A second drawer under the paper book shows the completed trades of the two advisory exit-on-extension lanes (the dashed curves), with exits labeled 'sold on extension' vs 'stop-out' — so the difference in selling behaviour is visible trade by trade. Advisory and one-regime, not the headline book.

title: Paper-book trade history
note: The paper book's drawer (Tracker page) can now show every completed trade: the day and price it was bought and sold, why it was sold, and what it made or lost in dollars of the display pot. Open positions gain a bought-date and P&L-so-far column. The table appears once the pipeline first exports its trade ledger; until then the band is unchanged.

title: Pre/post-market prices
note: During US extended hours the watchlist, market pulse, and today's-trade card now show live pre/post-market prices with a small PRE or POST tag, instead of sitting on the last regular close all evening. The change shown is measured against that close, matching what your broker displays. Brand-new listings without a prior close now show their live price with a dash for the change.

title: Clearer navigation
note: Every page now carries a full-width contents-style navigation bar, plus readability fixes: missing percentages show as a plain dash instead of '-%', the earnings scorecard's alpha columns are labeled again, and the day's trade idea sits under its own heading.

title: Whole-history calibration view
note: The Briefing's calibration band adds a full-history read alongside the 60-day window: older results fade gradually (90-day half-life) and thin samples are pulled toward the average, so there's no result cliff at day 60 and no lucky-streak scores. The math is spelled out in two new Terminology entries.

title: Clearer filter scope
note: The name filter now sits with the per-name drawers it actually filters. The headline scorecard always reads the full corpus — per-signal calibration is a property of the system, not of a hand-picked subset.

title: Trust meter on the tracker
note: The Signal Tracker now shows how close its own track record is to being trustworthy — how many market regimes we've seen and how many signals clear the bar — so a single-regime record can't be mistaken for proof.

title: Honest small-sample flags
note: Signal scorecards now flag when a number rests on too few results to trust, so a tidy percentage built on a handful of calls can't pose as a proven finding.

title: Simplified this tracker
note: Replaced three overlapping scoring tables with one plain scorecard plus this change log. The detailed per-name history now lives in a collapsed drawer.

title: Deterministic bucket routing
note: Which bucket a stock lands in (consider / accumulate / watch / avoid) is now decided by a fixed rule, not the AI — no more contradictions between a signal and where it was filed.

title: Trading-day guard
note: Over holidays and weekends, stale sessions are labeled 'already reported' instead of being silently shown again as if fresh.

title: Fewer false alarms
note: Retired internal checks that were wrong 40-60% of the time. Quieter, more trustworthy logs.

title: Cleaner performance math
note: Fixed how each call's return is compared against its benchmark so the comparison is apples-to-apples (matched to the same session's close).
